narrative:
Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your path toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your bar exam journey tipping toward a 51% chance of not passing, leading to this tough call on your application. Picture it like a story the model pieced together from your profile: your Year 3 grades landing you in the 4th decile at school stood out as the biggest chapter pulling toward failure, suggesting those later years brought tougher academic hurdles than hoped. Right behind that was your 5th decile in Year 1, reinforcing a pattern of mid-pack performance early on that the model saw as a steady undercurrent. Your middle family income bracket added another layer, as it often signals fewer resources for the intensive prep many successful bar takers lean on. Even with your strong 3.8 undergraduate GPA, your LSAT score of 27 didn't quite counterbalance that narrative for the model, painting a picture of uneven standardized test readiness. Being white and planning full-time studies nudged things further in that direction, aligning with patterns the model has seen where these factors sometimes correlate with added pressures without the edge others get. Your background as a female was a smaller thread, but still wove into the overall story of risk. It's not that any one piece defined you—your UGPA shows real promise—but together, they sketched a path where the bar exam felt like an uphill climb based on similar stories the model knows well. This isn't a judgment on your potential or heart; it's just the model's honest read on the odds. If you boost those deciles or LSAT in retakes, or tap extra support, the story could rewrite itself beautifully. You've got strengths here worth building on—keep pushing. With encouragement,  
Your Admissions Guide
